problem_statement: A volunteer-coordination web application's end-to-end suite intermittently reports that a signed-in coordinator is redirected to the login page while uploading a shift photo. The release was paused even though production uploads and direct API checks show no matching failure.

observations.statement: All 17 failures in the retained month occurred on parallel shard 3 after the offline-mode scenario had run earlier in the same shard; the same upload scenario passed 286 times on other shards.
The browser screenshot shows an offline banner for one frame before the redirect, while the server access log records no upload request or session-validation request for that attempt.
observations.provenance: retained screenshot sequence and server access log

observations.statement: The runner definition allows a browser profile directory to persist for the lifetime of a shard, and cleanup records only page closure rather than service-worker unregistration or cache deletion.
observations.provenance: archived runner definition and teardown log

observations.statement: A runner image refresh introduced both a new browser build and a proxy helper revision two days before the first failure; application source, API image, and test scenario source were unchanged.
observations.provenance: signed runner and application build manifests

observations.statement: Historical serial runs using a fresh profile for each scenario include 412 passes of the same upload path and no login redirect, providing a negative control for the stable application build and fixture account.

Production telemetry over the same interval contains 18,204 successful photo uploads and a stable authentication-refresh error rate, including traffic on the paused release candidate's backend revision.
Direct read-only contract checks retained from each failed run show that the fixture account session was valid and the upload endpoint returned healthy status from outside the browser path.
observations.provenance: archived preflight and postflight check results

observations.statement: The retained network archive starts after navigation and omits service-worker lifecycle events; proxy logs are overwritten per scenario and do not preserve whether an offline rule survived teardown.

The archived evidence cannot distinguish whether persisted browser state, a leaked proxy rule, or their interaction produced the redirect; resolving that distinction requires future per-scenario lifecycle and rule-state capture, not a production mutation.
